attributes.stem:
An otherwise well 65-year-old man undergoes repair of an obstructed right inguinal hernia. The operation is done through a groin approach and a loop of viable small bowel is found in the hernia sac and reduced. When reviewed three hours, later the patient has not passed urine and he does not feel as if he needs to. Apart from a moderate degree of pain associated with the procedure, he feels quite well and has tolerated a small volume of food and water.

His blood pressure is 150/80 mmHg, pulse rate 90/min and oxygen saturation is 94% breathing room air. The physical examination is otherwise normal. His bladder is not palpable. He has no other significant medical history.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is not in any particular discomfort following his hernia repair. He does not express any desire to need to void, and it has only been three hours since his operation. Quite understandably he does have some post-operative pain and in the absence of any other problems, this should be the current focus of care.

Oral analgesia is the most appropriate response. Considering the patient is still in moderate pain post-operatively, it is important to ensure appropriate analgesia. Additionally, it is possible that his ongoing pain is playing a role in him not passing urine due to concerns of worsening his pain, so this should be addressed. Once adequate analgesia has been provided he should try to void spontaneously. Non-opioid analgesia should be trialled first, as opioids can be associated with urinary retention. If he is still unable to void spontaneously, a bladder scan or ultrasound of the bladder should be performed to evaluate for urinary retention +/- catheter insertion.

Urethral bladder catheterisation is not the most appropriate response. There is no evidence that this man had a distended bladder or is in acute urinary retention. As such, this is not an appropriate intervention at this time - but may be needed if he has not voided between 4-6 hours post-operatively.

A 500 ml bolus of isotonic saline is not the most appropriate response. The patient is tolerating oral fluids currently, and he is not currently showing any signs suggestive of dehydration.

Oral prazosin is not the most appropriate response. Alpha recepter blockers are indicated in benign prostatic hyperplasia to improve urinary flow via relaxing smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck, however, this patient has no known history nor symptoms suggestive of benign prostatic hypertrophy (BPH). Further, selective alpha antagonists such as tamsulosin would be preferred in cases of BPH due to reduced adverse effects. There is currently no indication for this agent.

Intravenous furosemide is not the most appropriate response. Furosemide is indicated for oedema to off-load fluid excess. This patient is not currently showing any signs of being fluid overloaded. As such, this agent is not currently appropriate to use.
learningPoints: Post-operative care should be focussed on the relief of the patient's symptoms.
references: Jackson, J, Davies, P, Leggett, N., et al. (2019). Systematic review of interventions for the prevention and treatment of postoperative urinary retention. BJS open, 2019; 3:11-23.

Clancy C, Coffey JC, O'Riordain MG, et al. A meta-analysis of the efficacy of prophylactic alpha-blockade for the prevention of urinary retention following primary unilateral inguinal hernia repair. Am J Surg, 2018; 216:337-41.

Brouwer, T.A., van Roon, E.N., Rosier, P.F.W.M. et al. Postoperative urinary retention: risk factors, bladder filling rate and time to catheterization: an observational study as part of a randomized controlled trial. Perioper Med 10, 2 (2021). https://doi.org/10.1186/s13741-020-00167-z
